title: Bread and Butter Pudding

ingredients.name: white bread, preferably a day old

subs.name: brioche, panettone, or leftover pav — enriched breads make the pudding more luxurious
subs.notes: reduce butter as these are already rich

subs.name: all milk (use 2 cups total) — lighter but still delicious
subs.notes: the custard will be slightly less set

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: 2 tbsp in the custard, 2 tbsp for the top
ingredients.notes: 2 tbsp in the custard, 2 tbsp for the top

subs.name: a pinch of cardamom powder (elaichi) — transforms this into a lovely Anglo-Indian dessert
subs.notes: reduce to 0.25 tsp as cardamom is assertive

subs.name: sultanas, diced dried apricot, or chocolate chips — all work well
subs.notes: soak dried fruit in warm water 15 minutes first for plumpness

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 160°C. Grease a 20×25 cm baking dish with a little butter. Spread the remaining butter over one side of each bread slice. If the bread is very fresh, let the buttered slices air out for 10 minutes — slightly stale bread absorbs the custard more evenly.

Cut each buttered slice diagonally in half to make triangles. Arrange a single layer of bread in the baking dish (buttered side up), overlapping slightly. Scatter half the raisins and a pinch of cinnamon over this layer. Add the second layer of bread on top in the same overlapping pattern, buttered side up.

Whisk together the eggs, milk, cream, 2 tablespoons of sugar, vanilla, remaining cinnamon, nutmeg and salt until the sugar is dissolved and the mixture is fully combined. This is your custard — taste it; it should be pleasantly sweet and fragrant.

Pour the custard slowly and evenly over the bread, making sure every slice is well soaked. Gently press the top layer of bread down with the back of a spoon so it absorbs the liquid. Scatter the remaining raisins over the top. Let the dish rest for 10 minutes so the bread soaks fully before baking.

Sprinkle the remaining 2 tablespoons of sugar evenly over the surface. Place the dish in the preheated oven and bake for 35–40 minutes until the top is deep golden and slightly puffed, the custard is set around the edges but still has a gentle wobble in the centre.

Remove from the oven and rest for 10 minutes — the pudding will settle and the custard will firm up. Serve warm in scoops directly from the dish. It reheats well the next day with a splash of milk spooned over before warming.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bread → brioche, panettone, or leftover pav — enriched breads make the pudding more luxurious (reduce butter as these are already rich); cream → all milk (use 2 cups total) — lighter but still delicious (the custard will be slightly less set); raisin → sultanas, diced dried apricot, or chocolate chips — all work well (soak dried fruit in warm water 15 minutes first for plumpness); vanilla → a pinch of cardamom powder (elaichi) — transforms this into a lovely Anglo-Indian dessert (reduce to 0.25 tsp as cardamom is assertive)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: White Britannia sandwich bread or local sliced bread works perfectly. Use Amul butter, Amul cream, and vanilla essence (widely available in Indian baking stores). Without an oven, use a pressure cooker as a steam oven: grease the inner vessel, pour in the assembled pudding, seal with a plate (do not use the weight), place the cooker over a tawa on very low flame, and steam for 25–30 minutes until set — the top won't brown but the custard will be creamy and delicious. Add cardamom to the custard for an irresistible Indian touch.
